product_summary.one_sentence_summary: Remote EU-wide AI literacy workshop that translates AI Act obligations into practical staff habits and basic governance discipline.
product_summary.target_user: EU employers and deployers of AI systems, especially HR/L&D, compliance and business leaders managing multi-country teams.
product_summary.core_pain: They need a practical way to meet AI literacy expectations across countries and teams without relying on generic English-only content.
product_summary.claimed_outcome: Staff AI literacy, safer internal AI use and a more defensible compliance posture.

primary_buyer_type.reason: The workshop is still fundamentally a training purchase even though it has compliance implications.

secondary_buyer_types.reason: Compliance teams will care about AI literacy wording and policy alignment.
Business leaders may sponsor the product when AI usage is already happening across teams.

budget_owner.reason: Budget can sit in HR/L&D, compliance or business leadership depending on how the company frames the need.

buyer_influencers.reason: Training logistics and roll-out usually sit with HR/L&D.
AI literacy claims become stronger when compliance is involved.
Policy and claim wording should be checked by legal or governance teams.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: AI Act Article 4 requires providers and deployers of AI systems to take measures ensuring sufficient AI literacy of staff and other persons acting on their behalf.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: This is the core regulatory basis for the product.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: AI literacy measures should reflect technical knowledge, experience, education, training and the context in which AI systems are used.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: The Commission's AI literacy Q&A makes the context-sensitive interpretation explicit.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: House of Training offers practical AI fundamentals training that can substitute for generic AI literacy workshops.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: House of Training publicly lists a 4h face-to-face generative AI fundamentals course in French.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: Elements of AI is a free online substitute with no attendance requirements or exams.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: The course description states that it is completed online with no attendance requirements or exams.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: EU-wide coverage applies to all companies.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: The AI Act applies to providers and deployers of AI systems and related actors in scope, not to every company in the EU by default.

internal_claims_unverified.claim_text: Most AI literacy training is English-only.
internal_claims_unverified.reason_unverified: The reviewed substitutes include a French public offering and a multilingual corporate market example, so the blanket language claim is not proven.

internal_claims_unverified.claim_text: EU-wide remote delivery is enough to solve the buyer's AI literacy problem.
internal_claims_unverified.reason_unverified: The product may still need local policy and country-context adaptation.

internal_claims_unverified.claim_text: The multilingual FR/DE/EN angle is a durable geographic advantage.
internal_claims_unverified.reason_unverified: The advantage is plausible but not externally established.

regulator_entities.name: European Commission AI Office
regulator_entities.relevance: Publishes AI literacy guidance and interpretive material for Article 4.

regulator_entities.name: National market surveillance authorities
regulator_entities.relevance: Member State authorities are part of AI Act enforcement and oversight.

framework_entities.name: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (AI Act)
framework_entities.relevance: Creates the Article 4 AI literacy obligation.

framework_entities.name: AI Literacy - Questions & Answers
framework_entities.relevance: Clarifies interpretation, scope and context sensitivity for AI literacy obligations.

regulatory_requirements.requirement: Providers and deployers of AI systems must take measures to ensure a sufficient level of AI literacy of staff and other persons acting on their behalf.
regulatory_requirements.applies_to: AI-system providers and deployers in the EU

regulatory_requirements.requirement: AI literacy measures should be proportionate to the role, technical knowledge and context of the people using AI systems.
regulatory_requirements.applies_to: AI-system providers and deployers implementing Article 4

competitors_or_substitutes.name: House of Training - Generative artificial intelligences - Fundamentals
competitors_or_substitutes.notes: Public corporate AI fundamentals course with face-to-face delivery in French.

competitors_or_substitutes.name: Elements of AI / Building AI
competitors_or_substitutes.notes: Free online AI course with no attendance requirements or exams.

legal_claim_severity.reason: The product is adjacent to a compliance obligation, but it is still a training workshop rather than a licensed or accredited route.

authority_needed_for_delivery.reason: Practical AI usage plus policy guidance benefits from SME review, especially when distributed across countries.

authority_needed_for_claims.reason: Claims about compliance posture and AI literacy should be checked by legal or governance expertise.

proof_stack.reason: The product has to win against free and low-cost substitutes.
A paid pilot is the cleanest early signal that buyers prefer the managed workshop format.
Because the product is sold with compliance framing, defensibility of the content matters.
Staff understanding and safer use remain important intermediate signals.

proof_burden.reason: The market requires more than attendance; it wants defensible learning and compliance value.

dependency_types.reason: Practical AI usage, prompting and policy guidance should be reviewed for privacy and governance implications.
The product is EU-wide but still needs adaptation for local policies and languages.
Compliance-adjacent AI training benefits from legal or governance subject-matter input.

scope_variance_risk.reason: The regulation is EU-wide, but internal policy expectations and language needs vary by company and country.

expansion_variance_risk.reason: Expanding into new countries or industries would change policy and localization requirements materially.

subsidy_exists.reason: No direct public funding route was confirmed for this workshop.

subsidy_eligibility_status.reason: Country-level training aid may exist, but no EU-wide subsidy route was verified.

subsidy_conflict_flag.reason: No specific subsidy promise was made or verified.

market_regulatory_context.reason: The product is a training workshop, but it sits next to a real EU compliance obligation.

claim_language_risk.reason: Compliance wording should stay precise because the AI Act scope is narrower than 'all companies'.

authority_dependency_risk.reason: Claims about compliance posture need legal or governance validation.

competitive_gap_risk.reason: Public substitutes already exist, including a free online course and a corporate fundamentals course.

numeric_claim_risk.reason: The card's blanket all-company framing is materially misleading relative to the AI Act scope.

positioning_risk.reason: The product may be too close to generic AI literacy unless the compliance and localization layer is strong.

claim_conflicts.claim_text: EU-wide coverage applies to all companies.
claim_conflicts.what_is_wrong: The AI Act covers providers, deployers and other in-scope actors; it is not accurate to state that every EU company is automatically in scope.
The reviewed substitutes include a French corporate course and a free multilingual-style public alternative, so the language-market claim is not proven.

recommended_manual_review.reason: Reword the offer so it only claims Article 4 relevance for in-scope AI-system actors.
Decide which countries and policies are in scope for the first version of the workshop.
Keep the proof model focused on buyer acceptance and defensibility rather than attendance alone.

sources.title: Course Description: Elements of AI: Building AI Syllabus and Learning Objectives
sources.publisher: University of Helsinki / Elements of AI
